coredata.dc:title: Graphene near infrared-I/II probe in two-photon excitation-wavelength-independent photoluminescence and photoinactivation
coredata.dc:creator: Kuo, Wen-Shuo
Wu, Ping-Ching
Chang, Chia-Yuan
Wang, Jiu-Yao
Chen, Pei-Chi
Hsieh, Miao-Hsi
Lin, Sheng-Han
Cheng, Jen-Suo
Chou, Yu-Tsung
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doping and amino-group functionalization through chemical modification can engender enhance electron donation. Using a homemade femtosecond titanium:sapphire laser optical system operated at a low energy and short photoexcitation time [power: 200.0 nJ pixel−1; scanning frequency: 150 scans (∼0.80–1.33 s); excitation wavelength: 870, 910, or 970 nm], this study conducted these processes on the large π-conjugated system of graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based materials functioning as electron donors; this improved the efficiency of charge transfer to the prepared amino-N-GQDs, resulting in enhanced two-photon absorption, excitation-wavelength-independent photoluminescence (EWI-PL), radiative efficiency, excitation absolute cross section, and excitation from the near-infrared (NIR)-I to the NIR-II region. The lifetime decreased and quantum yield (QY) increased. The sorted amino-N-GQDs exhibited two-photon EWI-PL emissions between the ultraviolet and NIR-I regions, leading to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which functioned as two-photon photosensitizers for photodynamic therapy (PDT). Increasing the mean lateral size of the particles increased the N-functionality-dependent photochemical and electrochemical activities, which improved the PL QY and thus enhanced the efficiency of two-photon PDT. Additionally, a polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) coating was applied (forming sorted amino-N-GQD-PSS); the sulfur atoms introduced by the PSS induced the radiative recombination of localized electron–hole pairs and then improved the sorted amino-N-GQD surfaces by strengthening the quantum confinement of their emissive energy. Thus, compared with the sorted amino-N-GQDs that were not coated, the PSS-coated materials had superior two-photon properties. No ROS were detected. Accordingly, these materials are suitable for use as two-photon contrast probes for analyte tracking and localization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sorted graphene quantum dot
Excitation-wavelength-independent photoluminescence
Near-infrared-II excitation
Two-photon ultraviolet-near-infrared bioimaging
Two-photon photodynamic inactivation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation of negatively stained bacteria
Femtosecond laser optical system for the measurements of TPA and TPL
Characterization of materials and determination of EWI-PL phenomenon
Investigation of two-photon property and ROS assays under TPE
Determination of MDR bactericidal capability under two-photon PDT in the NIR-I/II windows
Sorted amino-N-GQD NIR-I/II probes in TPL bioimaging and EM images

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
HR-TEM images of (a) amino-N-GQD0.8, (b) amino-N-GQD6.4, and (c) amino-N-GQD10.1, which had diameters of 0.8 ± 0.1, 6.4 ± 0.2, and 10.1 ± 0.2 nm, respectively. The images depict graphene {1
1
−
00} lattice planes with a 0.213-nm d-spacing for the sorted amino-N-GQDs. The sorted materials exhibited different PL emissions at wavelengths encompassing the (a, inset) UV, (b, inset) visible to (c, inset) NIR windows.

Z-axis scan of a thin gold film used for measuring the second harmonic generation signal at different positions. The laser system's z-axis resolutions (full width at half maximum) were (a) 0.88, (b) 0.91, and (c) 0.93 μm (fit using the Gaussian function) for Ex. = 870, 910, and 970 nm, respectively. Relative TPA spectra of the sorted (d1) amino-N-GQDs and (d2) amino-N-GQD-PSS. TPE was executed as a function of wavelength (850–1000 nm) to monitor signals. (e1,e2) Viability (%) of material–Abprotein A-treated MRSA. The bacteria were found to be viable using the CFU assay at a selected TPE power of 200.0 nJ pixel−1 (amino-N-GQD0.8 and amino-N-GQD-PSS1.0: 150 scans and total effective exposure time of ∼0.80 s for Ex. of 870 nm; amino-N-GQD6.4 and amino-N-GQD-PSS6.7: total effective exposure time of ∼0.88 s for Ex. of 910 nm; amino-N-GQD10.1 and amino-N-GQD-PSS10.4: total effective exposure time of ∼1.00 s for Ex. of 970 nm). Regarding the amino-N-GQD0.8–, amino-N-GQD6.4–, and amino-N-GQD10.1–Abprotein A-treated MRSA subjected to TPE, p = 0.0609, p = 0.0335, and p < 0.0001, respectively; ∗p obtained through Student's t-test (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001). Delivered dose: OD600 ∼ 0.05 for MRSA and 0.5 μg mL−1 for material–Abprotein A. Data are mean ± standard deviation (n = 6). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Phosphorescence spectra at 1,270 nm (a) for amino-N-GQD0.8 and amino-N-GQD-PSS1.0 [number of scans: 100–200; total effective exposure time: 0.54–1.07 s; TPE power: 200.0 nJ pixel−1 (2.75 mW); Ex.: 870 nm], (b) for amino-N-GQD6.4 and amino-N-GQD-PSS6.7 [number of scans: 100–200; total effective exposure time: 0.59–1.17 s; TPE power: 200.0 nJ pixel−1 (3.10 mW); Ex.: 910 nm], (c) for amino-N-GQD10.1 and amino-N-GQD-PSS10.4 [number of scans: 100–200; total effective exposure time: 0.67–1.33 s; TPE power: 200.0 nJ pixel−1 (3.15 mW); Ex.: 970 nm], (d) for amino-N-GQD0.8 and amino-N-GQD-PSS1.0 [number of scans: 150; total effective exposure time: 0.88 s; TPE power 150.0 nJ pixel−1 (2.07 mW), 200.0 nJ pixel−1 (2.75 mW), and 250.0 nJ pixel−1 (3.44 mW); Ex.: 870 nm], (e) for amino-N-GQD6.4 and amino-N-GQD-PSS6.7 [number of scans: 150; total effective exposure time: 0.88 s; TPE power: 150.0 nJ pixel−1 (2.33 mW), 200.0 nJ pixel−1 (3.10 mW), and 250.0 nJ pixel−1 (3.87 mW); Ex.: 910 nm], and (f) for amino-N-GQD10.1 and amino-N-GQD-PSS10.4 [number of scans: 150; total effective exposure time: 1.00 s; TPE power: 150.0 nJ pixel−1 (2.37 mW), 200.0 nJ pixel−1 (3.15 mW), and 250.0 nJ pixel−1 (3.94 mW); Ex.: 970 nm]. Delivered dose = 0.5 μg mL−1 of the material.

(a) Relative PL spectra of materials for TPE (200.0 nJ pixel−1; Ex.: 870, 910, or 970 nm). (b1, b2) PL intensity versus excitation power (logarithm) of materials and fluorophores: TPE power from 14.6 mW (1,460 nJ pixel−1) to 54.6 mW (5,460 nJ pixel−1; Ex.: 870 nm); from 12.9 mW (1,290 nJ pixel−1) to 48.5 mW (4,850 nJ pixel−1; Ex.: 910 nm); and from 12.7 mW (1,270 nJ pixel−1) to 47.7 mW (4,770 nJ pixel−1; Ex.: 970 nm). R
2 > 0.999. (c1, c2) Time-resolved room-temperature PL decay profiles of materials (200.0 nJ pixel−1; Ex.: 870, 910, or 970 nm). Ex. was 870 nm for amino-N-GQD0.8 and amino-N-GQD-PSS1.0; 910 nm for amino-N-GQD6.4 and amino-N-GQD-PSS6.7; and 970 nm for amino-N-GQD10.1 and amino-N-GQD-PSS10.4.

TPL images acquired for (a1) unlabeled bacteria and (b1,c1,d1) material–Abprotein A-treated bacteria at a depth of 190 μm with 20 scans. (e1,f1,g1) Images acquired after an additional 150 scans. (h) TPAF image of unlabeled bacteria and (i) TPL image of collagen matrix alone. TEM images showing (a2) bare MRSA and (b2,c2,d2,e2,f2,g2) material–Abprotein A-treated MRSA with the same treatments as in (a1,b1,c1,d1,e1,f1,g1). Delivered dose: OD600 of ∼0.05 for MRSA; 0.5 μg mL−1 for material–Abprotein A; 200.0 nJ pixel−1 of TPE power. TPE wavelength = 870 nm for amino-N-GQD0.8, 910 nm for amino-N-GQD6.4, and 970 nm for amino-N-GQD10.1.

(a–c) TEM and (d–f) HAADF STEM images for bacteria treated with material–Abprotein A after photoexcitation (200.0 nJ pixel−1) and an additional 12 h of incubation. TPL images acquired for material–Abprotein A-treated bacteria at the depth of (g–i) 190 μm (TPE power of 150.0 pixel−1 with 150 scans) and (j–l) 250 μm (TPE power of 200.0 nJ pixel−1 with 150 scans). Delivered dose: OD600 of ∼0.05 for MRSA; 0.5 μg mL−1 for material–Abprotein A. TPE wavelength = 870 nm for amino-N-GQD-PSS1.0, 910 nm for amino-N-GQD-PSS6.7, and 970 nm for amino-N-GQD-PSS10.4.
Layout of the femtosecond laser optical imaging system.

row.entry: Ratio of radiative to nonradiative decay rates

head.ce:title: Graphene near infrared-I/II probe in two-photon excitation-wavelength-independent photoluminescence and photoinactivation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen doping and amino-group functionalization through chemical modification can engender enhance electron donation. Using a homemade femtosecond titanium:sapphire laser optical system operated at a low energy and short photoexcitation time [power: 200.0 nJ pixel−1; scanning frequency: 150 scans (∼0.80–1.33 s); excitation wavelength: 870, 910, or 970 nm], this study conducted these processes on the large π-conjugated system of graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based materials functioning as electron donors; this improved the efficiency of charge transfer to the prepared amino-N-GQDs, resulting in enhanced two-photon absorption, excitation-wavelength-independent photoluminescence (EWI-PL), radiative efficiency, excitation absolute cross section, and excitation from the near-infrared (NIR)-I to the NIR-II region. The lifetime decreased and quantum yield (QY) increased. The sorted amino-N-GQDs exhibited two-photon EWI-PL emissions between the ultraviolet and NIR-I regions, leading to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which functioned as two-photon photosensitizers for photodynamic therapy (PDT). Increasing the mean lateral size of the particles increased the N-functionality-dependent photochemical and electrochemical activities, which improved the PL QY and thus enhanced the efficiency of two-photon PDT. Additionally, a polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) coating was applied (forming sorted amino-N-GQD-PSS); the sulfur atoms introduced by the PSS induced the radiative recombination of localized electron–hole pairs and then improved the sorted amino-N-GQD surfaces by strengthening the quantum confinement of their emissive energy. Thus, compared with the sorted amino-N-GQDs that were not coated, the PSS-coated materials had superior two-photon properties. No ROS were detected. Accordingly, these materials are suitable for use as two-photon contrast probes for analyte tracking and localization.

ce:section.ce:para: Intrinsic-state and defective-state emissions may contribute to the photoluminescence (PL) emission of graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based materials and enable their application in biomedical research, such as that on imaging and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [1]. In general, atom doping is an effective method of modifying the photoproperties or other intrinsic properties of carbon-based materials, including their electronic characteristics and surface and local chemical features. Nitrogen or sulfur doping is extensively used to modify the intrinsic properties of graphene. Incorporating electron-accepting N or S atoms, which are of comparable atomic size to C atoms, into GQDs induce a high positive charge density at the adjacent C atoms in such dots [2]. For GQDs exhibiting notable quantum confinement and edge effects, bonded N or S atoms can alter the chemical composition and modulate the band gap of the GQDs; this thus engenders enhanced photochemical properties for tunable luminescence in bioimaging and efficient photodynamic action [3]. Furthermore, the chemical modification of primary amine molecules (also called amino-functionalization) results in strong electron donation, which affects the electronic properties of GQD-based materials considerably and thus enhances their photochemical and electrochemical activities [4].

However, quantum confinement in sp2 domains, zigzag edge sites, and surface defect states may be involved in size-dependent and excitation-wavelength-dependent PL (EWD-PL) [5]. To fabricate materials with tunable photochemical properties, GQD-based materials exhibiting excitation-wavelength-independent (EWI)-PL emissions can be sieved to obtain sorted GQD-based materials with homogeneous atom dopant functionalities. Such materials can be used to investigate intrinsic and electronic properties related to optical behaviors caused by quantum confinement effects. A study conjugated sorted GQD-based materials with N-doped, amino-functionalized, and S-atom-containing polymers (sorted amino-N-GQD-based material–polymer), thus engendering quantum confinement effects and inducing enhanced PL quantum yield (QY) [4]. Another study reported that the singlet–triplet splitting of amino-N-GQDs could enhance the efficiency of intersystem crossing; the reason for this is that intersystem crossing competes with internal conversion among states with the same multiplicity and considerably enhances the photochemical characteristics of the materials [6]. Moreover, a study revealed that sorted materials exhibited notable two-photon properties [5]. Accordingly, sorted materials have potential for use as two-photon contrast agents or photosensitizers to facilitate exploration processes (or phenomena).

In this study, highly crystalline and uniform sorted amino-N-GQDs were prepared and then examined. The examination revealed that the sorted amino-N-GQDs exhibited EWI-PL emissions between the ultraviolet (UV) and near-infrared (NIR) regions after two-photon excitation (TPE). Increasing the mean lateral size of the sorted amino-N-GQDs resulted in favorable two-photon absorption (TPA), two-photon luminescence (TPL), two-photon QY, two-photon absolute cross sections, two-photon lifetimes, and two-photon radiative decay rates. Furthermore, reactive oxygen species (ROS) were generated for use in two-photon photodynamic inactivation (or PDT) to enhance the antimicrobial efficiency of the sorted dots at a low TPE energy and short photoexcitation time. As the size of the sorted amino-N-GQDs was increased, the concentration of N functionalities increased, inducing the radiative recombination of localized electron–hole pairs. Thus, the sorted amino-N-GQDs have potential for use as a two-photon contrast probe for tracking and localizing analytes through in-depth two-photon imaging (TPI) in a three-dimensional (3D) biological environment; they also have potential for use in two-photon PDT to easily eliminate multidrug-resistant (MDR) species. Additionally, surface coating with polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) introduced S, which boosted the cooperative effect (among the electrons captured by the new surface states) on the heteroatom-doped materials; this can be attributed to the crosslink-enhanced emission [7,8]. This enhanced the frequency of radiative recombination. Therefore, surface coating with PSS (to create sorted amino-N-GQD-PSS) improved the surface of the sorted amino-N-GQDs by strengthening the quantum confinement of their emission energy. Consequently, the two-photon properties of the coated materials were modified to a greater extent than those of the sorted materials that were not coated. Moreover, no ROS were detected when the coating was applied, rendering the sorted amino-N-GQD-PSS suitable for use as two-photon contrast probes for the tracking and localization of analytes in a 3D environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation of negatively stained bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria were picked up from colonies and suspended in 1% aqueous sodium phosphotungstate solution (Sigma Aldrich Co., USA) at pH 7.0. Droplets of the suspensions were allowed to dry on grids coated with Formvar. The samples were then subjected to TEM observation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Femtosecond laser optical system for the measurements of TPA and TPL
ce:section.ce:para: A novel inverted optical microscopy system with a femtosecond Ti-sapphire laser [a repetition rate of 80 MHz; Mai Tai with the optical parametric oscillators (OPO), Spectra-Physics, USA] optical system: an inverted optical microscope (Leica, Germany), a x–y galvanometer scanner (USA), a triple-axis sample-positioning stage (Prior Scientific Instruments Ltd., UK), a z–axis piezoelectric nano-positioning stage (Mad City Labs, USA), photomultiplier tubes (Hamamatsu, Japan), and a data acquisition card with a field-programmable gate array module (National Instruments, USA) (Scheme 1 ).
Complete experimental section for this article can be found in the Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of materials and determination of EWI-PL phenomenon
Investigation of two-photon property and ROS assays under TPE
ce:section.ce:para:
Nonlinear multiphoton laser microscopy (or two-photon laser microscopy) is typically used in tandem with laser excitation in the NIR region to maximize tissue transmission [11] and is considered an alternative approach to achieving photochemical action at an ultralow energy level and short photoexcitation duration [12]. This study conducted multiphoton laser microscopy by using a homemade femtosecond titanium:sapphire laser optical system (Mai Tai system with optical parametric oscillators, Spectra-Physics, USA; Scheme 1) with a repetition rate of 80 MHz. The z-axis focal resolution of the laser system was determined to be approximately 0.88 μm at an excitation wavelength of 870 nm (Fig. 2 a), and the x−y-axis focal resolution was determined to be approximately 0.41 μm (for the calculations, please refer to the Supplementary Information). In theory, according to the diffraction equation, the focal spot behind the optical objective is larger when the excitation wavelength is longer. In this study, at an excitation wavelength of 910 nm, the x−y-axis and z-axis focal spot resolutions were approximately 0.43 and 0.91 μm, respectively, and at an excitation wavelength of 970 nm, these resolutions were approximately 0.46 and 0.93 μm, respectively (Fig. 2a–c). The most efficient excitation wavelengths for amino-N-GQD0.8, amino-N-GQD6.4, and amino-N-GQD10.1 under TPE were approximately 870, 910, and 970 nm, respectively (Fig. 2d1), which may be attributed to the interband transitions involved [13], referred to as TPA. In addition, the sorted N-GQD materials were coated with chemical reagents (SOSG, t-MVP, XTT, GSH, and H2DCFDA) because of their ability to generate ROS from these materials, thus enhancing the PDT efficiency; α-tocopherol was used to verify the involvement of the generated ROS in PDT. ROS such as singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide radical anion (O2
.− ), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) play major roles in PDT. Therefore, this study explored the signals of 1O2, O2
.− , and H2O2 produced after the laser irradiation of the materials to determine ROS action; this exploration was conducted at a material dose of 0.5 μg mL−1 by using photoexcitation (scanning frequency: 100–200 scans) and photoexcitation-power-dependent scanning (150.0–250.0 nJ pixel−1). The photoexcitation parameters are outlined as follows: for amino-N-GQD0.8, amino-N-GQD6.4, and amino-N-GQD10.1, the total effective exposure times were 0.54–1.07.0.59–1.17, and 0.67–1.33 s, respectively; excitation wavelengths were 870, 910, and 970 nm, respectively; and TPE power was approximately 200.0 nJ pixel−1, with the individual power levels being 2.75, 3.10, and 3.15 mW, respectively (Table S3; for the calculations, refer to the Supplementary Information). The photoexcitation-power-dependent scanning parameters are outlined as follows: for amino-N-GQD0.8, amino-N-GQD6.4, and amino-N-GQD10, the scanning frequencies were 150, 100, and 100 scans, respectively; total effective exposure times were 0.80, 0.88, and 1.00 s, respectively; and excitation wavelengths were 870, 910, and 970 nm, respectively. Furthermore, for the photoexcitation-power-dependent scanning process, the TPE power levels were approximately 150.0 nJ pixel−1 (2.07 mW), 200.0 nJ pixel−1 (2.75 mW), and 250.0 nJ pixel−1 (3.44 mW) for amino-N-GQD0.8; approximately 150.0 nJ pixel−1 (2.33 mW), 200.0 nJ pixel−1 (3.10 mW), and 250.0 nJ pixel−1 (3.87 mW) for amino-N-GQD6.4; and approximately 150.0 nJ pixel−1 (2.37 mW), 200.0 nJ pixel−1 (3.15 mW), and 250.0 nJ pixel−1 (3.94 mW) for amino-N-GQD10.1 (Table S4; for the calculations, refer to the Supplementary Information). To prevent ROS production in the event of inadvertent material exposure to white light (which would have compromised our experiments), the PDT-related experiments were executed in the dark. The study revealed that large concentrations of ROS were generated. Additionally, the signals of 1O2 phosphorescence emitted from the sorted amino-N-GQDs at 1,270 nm exhibited a similar trend (Fig. 3 ). The estimated 1O2 QY (ΦΔ) was 0.23 for amino-N GQD0.8, 0.29 for amino-N-GQD6.4, and 0.34 for amino-N-GQD10.1 [for reference, ΦΔ = 0.64 for meso-tetra(4-sulfonatophenyl)porphine dihydrochloride dissolved in D2O [14]].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MDR bactericidal capability under two-photon PDT in the NIR-I/II windows
ce:section.ce:para:
The increasing prevalence of MDR bacterial strains is a major challenge in the treatment of hospital-acquired bacterial infections. This is because clinical infections are widespread and because antibiotics used to inhibit bacterial survival have been abused, leading to further antibiotic resistance; for example, the inordinate prescription of antibiotics has driven the expansion of bacterial resistance to β-lactam antibiotic groups [15]. The mutation of bacteria increases the difficulty of developing effective antibiotics; therefore, investigations must be conducted to discover new antimicrobial strategies and distinguish therapeutic access [16]. MDR bacteria, including the antibiotic-resistant gram-positive bacterium methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), are susceptible only to glycopeptide antimicrobials such as vancomycin and teicoplanin. In hospital-associated infections, pathogenic MRSA is often associated with such diseases as wound infections, septic arthritis, and endocarditis [15,16]. In addition to the use of chemicals to destroy neoplastic cells or microbes, PDT is a potential therapeutic approach for eliminating malignant tumors, cancers, MDR bacterial strains, and other microbes under laser irradiation. To determine the sustainable potential of the proposed materials as two-photon photosensitizers, MRSA was used in this study. The surface protein, namely protein A, on the cell wall of MRSA was considered. A material was coated with an Abprotein A antibody to form a material–Abprotein A hybrid to enhance specificity and efficiency; another material without an antibody coating was used for comparison. Although considerable quantities of 1O2, O2
.− , and H2O2 were generated, false-positive ROS signals may have been present; such signals could have been caused by interactions between the amino-N-GQDs and SOSG, t-MVP, XTT, GSH, and H2DCFDA and might have compromised our results. The quantities of ROS produced by the laser-irradiated material, namely Abprotein A-treated MRSA, were determined (Tables S5–S6), and the results were consistent with those obtained from the observation of ROS action (Tables S3–S6) and with the measured ΦΔ values. The material without antibody coating (Tables S7–S8) generated a lower quantity of ROS than did that with the Abprotein A coating. Furthermore, because lipopolysaccharide (LPS) is a major component of the outer membrane of gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli), a sorted dot–AbLPS hybrid was also able to eliminate E. coli (Tables S9–S12), exhibiting a trend similar to those presented in Tables S3–S8. This finding demonstrates that the materials had been successfully coated, resulting in enhanced specificity, selectivity, and efficiency.

The materials were assumed to be able to generate ROS to enhance the efficiency of PDT—executed at a selected TPE power of approximately 200.0 nJ pixel−1 (scanning frequency: 150 scans; total effective exposure time: 0.80, 0.88, and 1.00 s for excitation wavelengths of 870, 910, and 970 nm, respectively)—against MRSA (OD600: 0.05). To test this assumption, the system was photoexcited in the dark at 37 °C, and the bacteria treated with the photoexcited materials were subjected to 3 h of additional incubation to facilitate ROS generation for PDT. The number of surviving bacteria was calculated as the number of colony-forming units (CFU) per milliliter and is expressed as a percentage. The results revealed an approximately 0 log10 reduction, corresponding to nearly 100% viability, thus indicating favorable biocompatibility (Fig. S13). Upon laser exposure, the materials had no bactericidal effect on MRSA when used alone (Fig. 2e1); additionally, the sorted amino-N-GQDs exerted no bactericidal effects when laser treatment was not applied. However, the materials exhibited excellent bactericidal effects under TPE; specifically, approximately 67.3%, 79.6%, and 100% elimination was achieved for amino-N-GQD0.8–Abprotein A, amino-N-GQD6.4–Abprotein A, and amino-N-GQD10.1–Abprotein A, respectively, equivalent to approximately 0.49, 0.69, and 7.81 log10 reductions, respectively (Fig. 2e1; Fig. S14a). The bacterial viability was higher for the materials without an antibody coating than for those with an antibody coating (90.1%, 85.9%, and 80.3% vs. 32.7%, 20.4%, and 0%). Furthermore, the sorted dot–AbLPS exhibited efficient bactericidal effects on E. coli (Fig. S15), a similar trend to that observed for the sorted dot–Abprotein A-treated MRSA (Fig. 2e1; Fig. S14a). As the number of edge sites increased, the concentrations of C–N and N functionalities increased; in addition, as the sorted materials’ surfaces improved, the antibacterial ability and ROS quantity increased, leading to highly efficient PDT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sorted amino-N-GQD NIR-I/II probes in TPL bioimaging and EM images
ce:section.ce:para: For GQD-based materials, surface groups may contribute to a phenomenon associated with intrinsic and defect-state emissions that are involved in the PL mechanism. In this study, the size of the sorted amino-N-GQDs was increased, and Fig. 4 a displays the TPL spectra of these materials; TPE-associated red-shifted peaks were observed at 375, 562, and 773 nm, indicating a trend similar to that of the emission wavelength derived through one-photon excitation (Fig. S9). Subsequently, a two-photon process was confirmed because the PL intensity in this process was observed to be quadratically dependent on the TPE power [17], and the corresponding exponent was approximately 2.00 ± 0.02 (Fig. 4b1). Furthermore, TPE-associated EWI-PL emissions were noted. This property means that the materials can serve as an efficient tool for TPI. The sorted amino-N-GQDs exhibited a large absolute cross section [approximately 45,732–58,075 Goeppert–Mayer units (GM), with 1 GM = 10−50 cm4s photon−1 and Rhodamine B serving as a reference [18]] and high absorbed energy–to–input energy flux ratio in the biospecimens, which were favorable [19] for effective imaging (Table 1; Table S13). Additionally, amino-N-GQD0.8 had an average lifetime of approximately 1.25 ns (calculated from the following lifetime estimates: 0.21, 0.97, and 3.89 ns). By contrast, amino-N-GQD6.4 and amino-N-GQD10.1 had average lifetimes of approximately 1.14 and 1.06 ns, respectively (Fig. 4c1; Table S14). Therefore, the radiative–to–nonradiative decay rate ratio for amino-N GQD0.8 was approximately 0.27 (derived from observed rates of 1.68 × 108 and 6.32 × 108 s−1), whereas those for amino-N-GQD6.4 and amino-N-GQD10.1 were approximately 0.49 (2.89 × 108 to 5.88 × 108 s−1) and 0.67 (3.77 × 108 to 5.66 × 108 s−1; Table 1), respectively, revealing that the materials mainly passed through the radiative pathway when the QY was high and lifetime was low. Because a homemade optically inverted microscopy system is unsuitable for use in vivo assay processes, MRSA was embedded in a collagen matrix to mimic a 3D biological environment [20,21]. For the EWI-PL emitted from the sorted amino-N-GQDs with favorable two-photon properties, target bacteria can be tracked and localized through in-depth TPI. The TPL emitted from the sorted amino-N-GQD–Abprotein A-treated MRSA (Fig. 5b1, 5c1, and 5d1) at a TPE power of approximately 200.0 nJ pixel−1, scanning frequency of 20 scans (amino-N-GQD0.8: ∼0.11 s for 870 nm; amino-N-GQD6.4: ∼0.12 s for 910 nm; and amino-N-GQD10.1: ∼0.13 s for 970 nm), and depth of 190 μm revealed clear TPI results; the corresponding TEM images indicated that a substantial amount of material was adsorbed on the bacterial surface (Fig. 5b2, 5c2, and 5d2). However, the TPI of bacteria treated with the noncoated amino-N-GQDs demonstrated that the materials were only minimally attached to the bacterial surface (Fig. S16). By contrast, unlabeled bacteria were clearly observed under electron microscopy (Fig. 5a2), whereas bacteria were absent in the TPL image (Fig. 5a1). After an additional 150 scans (amino-N-GQD0.8: ∼0.80 s for 870 nm; amino-N-GQD6.4: ∼0.88 s for 910 nm; and amino-N-GQD10.1: ∼1.00 s for 970 nm), the TPL decreased as a result of PDT (Fig. 5e1, 5f1,and 5g1); this indicates changes in the bacterial cell wall or oxidation and signifies that the deterioration of the surrounding biological surface substrate had been induced by the generated ROS (Fig. 5e2, 5f2, and 5g2). These changes may have caused bacterial atrophy, distortion, and morphological damage, revealing that the materials had desorbed from the bacterial surface. Additionally, in the unlabeled bacteria, the two-photon autofluorescence of the intrinsic fluorophores was not observed at the same TPE power (Fig. 5h) or in the collagen matrix (Fig. 5i).

Although the sorted amino-N-GQD-based materials exhibited considerable advantages, they were not considered to be an excellent candidate for use as a contrast probe because of concerns regarding their practicability and stability. In general, the quantum confinement of energy within the surfaces of C-based materials can be strengthened through surface passivation; enhanced quantum confinement engenders a relatively high PL and QY in such materials. Furthermore, the generated ROS were probably inhibited by the polymer coating, which diminished the PDT action. Accordingly, the sorted amino-N-GQD surfaces were painted with PSS through an electrostatic interaction to investigate the materials’ photochemical properties. The relevant characterizations are presented in Figs. S17–S19. Raman spectroscopy revealed that the sorted amino-N-GQD-polymer had sizes of approximately 1.0 nm (amino-N-GQD-PSS1.0), 6.7 nm (amino-N-GQD-PSS6.7), and 10.4 nm(amino-N-GQD-PSS10.4), indicating a slight increase in diameter (Fig. S20; for calculations, refer to the Supplementary Information). According to the relative maximum ratio, amino-N-GQD-PSS1.0, amino-N-GQD-PSS6.7, and amino-N-GQD-PSS10.4 had satisfactory TPA at 870, 910, and 970 nm, respectively, under TPE (Fig. 2d2). For amino-N-GQD-PSS1.0, amino-N-GQD-PSS6.7, and amino-N-GQD-PSS10.4, the absolute QY levels were 0.30, 0.41, and 0.49, respectively [10]; absolute cross sections were approximately 46,914, 53,667, and 59,852 GM, respectively; average lifetimes were approximately 1.15, 1.07, and 0.92 ns, respectively (Fig. 4c2); and radiative–to–nonradiative decay rate ratios were approximately 0.43, 0.69, and 0.96, respectively (Table 1). These findings are consistent with the observations of another study, suggesting that the phenomenon can be triggered by the surface passivation of polymers containing S [22]. Surface passivation can enhance the integrity of surface π electron networks and inhibit the nonradiative recombination of localized electron–hole pairs, raising the QY of materials [23]. In this study, surface passivation efficiently increased the QY of the sorted amino-N-GQD-based materials. Additionally, when the treatments presented in Tables S3–S8 and S9–S12 were employed, the quantity of ROS decreased considerably (Tables S15–S17 and S18–S19), and a phosphorescence signal was observed at 1,270 nm (Fig. 3, highlighted in light red). However, the intensity was too low to be detected [ΦΔ = 0.01, 0.01, and 0.02 for amino-N-GQD-PSS1.0, amino-N-GQD-PSS6.7, and amino-N-GQD-PSS10.4, respectively; for reference, ΦΔ = 0.64 for meso-tetra(4-sulfonatophenyl)porphine dihydrochloride dissolved in D2O [14]]. In the bacteria treated with the photoexcited materials, the CFU experiments revealed an approximately 0 log10 reduction within the standard deviation, corresponding to an estimated 100% viability (Fig. 2e2; Fig. S14b and S15e–f). These results indicate that ROS inhibition occurred after PSS coating. TEM revealed that the physiological functions of the bacteria were maintained through external ion filtering and nutrient assimilation by the cell wall. However, any adsorbed material functioned as a barrier on the bacterial surface. The MRSA treated with the photoexcited amino-N-GQD-polymer materials exhibited no exceptional morphology, even after photoexcitation and an additional 12 h of incubation (Fig. 6 a–c). Furthermore, high-angle annular dark field scanning TEM (HAADF STEM) was used to determine the form of the amino-N-GQD-polymer nanoshell (Fig. 6d–f). The TPL spectra (Fig. 4a) of the sorted amino-N-GQD-PSS, which was verified as having a two-photon process (amino-N-GQD-PSS1.0: 389 nm; amino-N-GQD-PSS6.7: 570 nm; and amino-N-GQD-PSS10.4: 785 nm; exponent: ∼2.00 ± 0.02; Fig. 4b2), revealed a UV-to-NIR shift. The polymer-containing materials had superior PL and an absolute cross section (Table 1; Table S9) compared with the polymer-free materials, but the TPE power was reduced to approximately 25% (Fig. 6g–i; TPE power: 150.0 nJ pixel−1; scanning frequency: 150; depth: 190 μm) to maintain constant TPL intensity compared with that depicted in Fig. 5e1–5g1. In the corresponding TPI, clear images revealing bacterial localization were obtained; moreover, images could be obtained at a depth of 250 μm, with a TPE power of approximately 200.0 nJ pixel−1 and 150 scans (Fig. 6j–l). By contrast, the TPI of the bacteria treated with the antibody-coated materials revealed minimal attachment and nonspecific binding under the same conditions (Fig. S21). TPI could not be effectively executed at a depth of 275 μm after material–Ab treatment (Fig. S22). Studies on the crosslink-enhanced emission effect [7,24] have reported the existence of nonradiative traps and structures on the C core of a crystalline sorted GQD-based material with multiple crystalline layers. Moreover, the PSS coating was observed to be a potential fluorophore. Specifically, the linked PSS coated on the materials caused diminished rotation and vibration and thus enhanced the PL of the materials [23]. In such circumstances, a high QY is expected. The introduction of S and N atoms could enhance the cooperative effect (among electrons captured by new surface states) exerted on the properties of the heteroatom-doped materials. This could increase the frequency of radiative recombination and thus boost the QY [25]. Accordingly, the surface coating improved the surface of the sorted amino-N-GQDs by strengthening the quantum confinement of their emissive energy. These results reveal that the exceptionally bright, highly photostable, and sorted amino-N-GQD-PSS materials prepared in this study are suitable for use as fluorescence probes in biological and biomedical applications for target analysis.

A functional material that can be easily controlled is desirable. Sorted amino-N-GQDs measuring 0.8–10.1 nm in size and exhibiting EWI-PL from UV to NIR-I region were prepared through TPE in NIR-I or NIR-II window (via employment of a home-made femtosecond Ti-sapphire laser optical system). The as-prepared materials exhibited excellent generation of ROS for elimination of MDR microbes through two-photon PDT; thus, the materials could function as a two-photon photosensitizer. When the particle size of the GQDs was increased, the numbers of N functionalities increased, and a superior photochemical effect was achieved. Additionally, the S atoms introduced from PSS boosted the cooperative effect (among electrons captured by new surface states) of the heteroatom-doped materials. This enhanced radiative recombination and thus increased two-photon properties such as EWI-PL, QY, lifetime, and absolute cross section. By contrast, ROS inhibition occurred when material was coated with PSS. Therefore, surface coating resulted in improved amino-N-GQD surfaces because of the strengthened quantum confinement of their emissive energy. With a ready control process, the sorted amino-N-GQD-PSS can be used in contrast probes for tracking and localizing analytes in in-depth TPI in a biological environment.

Wen-Shuo Kuo: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Project administration, Investigation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization. Ping-Ching Wu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Software, Validation, Visualization. Chia-Yuan Chang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Resources, Software, Validation, Visualization. Jiu-Yao Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Resources, Software, Validation, Visualization. Pei-Chi Chen: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization. Miao-Hsi Hsieh: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript. Sheng-Han Lin: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Project administration, Investigation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization. Jen-Suo Cheng: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Project administration, Investigation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization. Yu-Tsung Chou: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Project administration, Investigation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by An Nan Hospital, China Medical University, Taiwan (ANHRF110-33); State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, Guangxi Normal University, China (CMEMR2021-B11); Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST 110-2221-E-006-013-MY3; MOST 109-2636-E-006-018-); Academia Sinica Healthy Longevity Grand Challenge Competition, Taiwan (AS-HLGC-110-07); Allergy Immunology and Microbiome Center, China Medical University Children's Hospital/ China Medical University Hospital, China Medical University, Taiwan (1JA8); E-Da Hospital, Taiwan.
